speaker: interviewee
text: Today is February 21st, 1997. I am interviewing the survivor Michael Jackson. And my name is Jeannie Miller. We are in Allentown, Pennsylvania, in the United States of America. And the language we're using is English. Today is February 21st, 1997. The survivor we are interviewing is Michael Jackson. And I am Jeannie Miller. We are interviewing in Allentown, Pennsylvania in the United States of America, and the language we're using is English.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now we have the red light on.

speaker: interviewee
text: What is your name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Michael Jackson. I was born in Torun, formerly Czechoslovakia. Torun was located--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, before we go into that, would you just spell your name, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mi-- M-I-C-H-A-E-L J-A-C-K-S-O-N. Michael Jackson. I think I should mention my original name, formerly, was Michael Jakubovics. J-A-K-U-B-O-V-I-C-S.

speaker: interviewee
text: And you said that your name-- your place of birth was Torun. What is your age now?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born on November 18, 1920. I am 77 years of age-- going to be in November.

speaker: interviewee
text: Tell us about your early life as a child.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in Torun. My parents named-- my mother-- father's name was Abraham. My mother's name was Rivka. I was the fourth child that they had. When I was born, my father was almost 50 years old. My mother was a year younger, about 49 or maybe 48. And my very oldest brother, his name was Eliezer. He was born in 1897. Next to him, my brother Moshe was born in 1899. And my other brother, that was the closest to me, was born and 1907. When I was born after World War I, this area in the Carpathian-- in the Carpathian belonged to the former Czechoslovakia. Torun was located exactly at the border between Poland and Czechoslovakia. That was the last village. On the other side of the mountain was already Poland, and the next village was called Wyszkow, which was Poland.

speaker: interviewee
text: What was your religious life like?

speaker: interviewee
text: The real religious life in these areas was, as I mentioned already to you yesterday, a Hassidic-- Hassidic, very observant of religious life. In Torun, we had 135 Jewish families. The village consisted roughly of about 3,000 people, and 135 families were roughly 800 souls. That included [? Lopeshna, ?] [? Shora, ?] and [? Prezla. ?] This was like three streets-- or three sides of a-- of a mountain. That was considered Torun. We had in Torun three synagogues, two-- two Mikvahs, and there was a Hebrew school in Torum. And this-- this was a unique-- unique school, because in the entire Carpathian region, in small places like Torun, there were no Hebrew schools. The only place that had a Hebrew school was cities like Munkacs and Chust or Sevlus. The-- and also, they-- the schools that they had became were organized later. The school-- and the Hebrew school in Torum for some reason was already, in 1920, in existence. It was, in general, like I said the-- the public schools was Ruthenian and Czech. And most-- most of the population in the small villages-- because villages like Torun were dotted all the way to the this city of Chust, which was west of Torun about 87 kilometers. Some had more or less between 2,000 and 3,000 populations. The Jewish population varied. And the next village, which was only four kilometers from Torun, was called Brzesc. And they only had about 100 Jewish families. But the interesting thing about it was that in Brzesc, there was no Zionist movement. And in Torun, there was a Zionist organization, the [HEBREW]. And they were the ones that-- with the help-- I think they did get help from the American Joint Distribution Committee to organize the Hebrew school in Torun. In general, the children went to public school a half a day. And after they went out from public school, they went to cheder That that was the-- the-- the education that they got. And that was the main-- the main emphasis that people had for education. It was not the secular education, but the Jewish education-- the Hebrew-- and of course, that higher education was already the Yeshivas, which some of them were very, very famous. The most famous one was like the one in [? Boeinghart ?] that produced great scientists, leaders, and also-- and also poets. The poet Avigdor Hameiri, he changed his name from-- Firestone was his name. He changed it to Hebrew. Hameiri was one of the students in this here-- in this famous Yeshiva. Also-- also, a Zionist leader by the name of Dr. Josef Potter-- he was also a student at that Yeshiva. And that my very oldest brother, Eliezer also was a student at that Yeshiva. And I know, an uncle of mine-- my mother's younger brother-- his name was Svee and mother's name Drimmer. He was also a student in that Yeshiva. But he left the Yeshiva, and acquired a secular education. He changed his name from Drimmer to Darvas, and his first name from Svee, which was Herman, to Armin. And he became, later, a high school teacher. And that's-- we'll talk about him later. I was hiding out in his place later.

speaker: interviewee
text: What was your level of education that you were able to reach?

speaker: interviewee
text: My level of education was to-- to go to-- we went to-- to cheder, of course, and finished the public school, which was 8-- 8 grades. One year-- one year the Hebrew school was not able to exist, because they didn't have enough money. So I went one year to a Ukrainian school-- Russian. I learned Cyril-- the Cyril language-- and later on, went to Czech school. There wasn't enough students, because the Czech-- [INAUDIBLE]. The Czechs only had-- they'd send in administrative people. They-- they-- the majority of the people were Ukrainians. They called them small Russians. And about a little bit better than 10% were the Jewish-- the Jewish people. So when I finished that public school, and I became a bar mitzvah, I wanted to go to a high school. But this-- there was no high school around. And it was around in the city of Munkacs. And this was 1933, at the depression time. My parents could not afford to send me to-- to high school, because you had to pay tuition and upkeep. My father had a sister that lived in the city-- in [? Noitserlich ?] the Hungarians called it. [? Noitserlich. ?] The Jews called it [? Serlich, ?] Salich-- there was a Yeshiva organized by the Supreme [? Carraba, ?] which was-- the Yeshiva's name was [HEBREW].

speaker: interviewer
text: So after my bar mitzvah, I was sent into that Yeshiva. Because in the same city, my father had that sister lived there. So I was-- I had where to stay. I stayed with my-- with my uncle and aunt there. And the Yeshiva happened to be just a block away. And so it was very convenient for me to be close there. And I-- and I stayed there for two semesters. After I finished two semesters, I grew up-- within one year-- as tall as I am now.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I came back sick from the city. And my parents took me to-- to the doctor. And I was very thin, and tall, and he suspected that I might get TB. And he advised my parents not to send me to the-- back to the city. Stay here, and-- which was in the country, like very fresh air. So my parents hired two tutors to continue my Yeshiva education in Torun. One of them-- the one of them happened to be a teacher that was the same age as my older brother-- older than I was-- about 25 years older. And the rabbi's son that was a scholar and in Talmud. And that-- I continued-- they continued to teach me. Every day I had three hours' tutoring. But the good fortune was that we had, in Torun, a library. At the Hebrew-- the Hebrew school had a library. And-- and the library-- it wasn't common in-- in-- in the small villages to-- to have libraries. In our libraries, we had books that were from classical writers translated into Yiddish. At that time, and in the city of [? Vilna ?] in Lithuania, the Jewish organization by the name of [? Yeda Yevoh-- ?] that exists now in New York City, part of it-- which is the acronym [YIDDISH] Institute. They-- they've actually reorganized the Yiddish language, because the language was chaotic. In each area, everybody wrote, it had no grammar.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they organized the language. And they also translated the most famous works of literary classical writers like Tolstoy, Gorky and Pushkin, Byron-- I remember [? mappa sohn, ?] and for instance, reading in Yiddish, or from Tolstoy, and others. So this was not-- you couldn't find that in every village in the Carpathian. It was unique.

speaker: interviewee
text: I told you that I just got that book that was published in Israel. And it writes about the neighboring village-- which was only four kilometers-- Bystre. And it says there that even though they were only for kilometers from Torun, they had very little influence by the Zionist movement that was in Torun. There were a few that left even from there-- but very few. Torun-- the Torun youth emigrated. That's after the war. Out of the 800 people that lived there, after the war we had over 100 survivors. And this was a large percentage of survivors. And the reason for it was because of the Zionist movement was strong in Torun . And a lot of them, my own-- from my own family-- cousins-- five of my own cousins left already in 1928, '29, and '30-- 1930. And that's how they survived. And also, there was a very famous rich family by the name of Moshe Svelansky. And he was a writer in a firm. In 1929, he knew about the area-- the Carpathian-- where the people-- as I said, they were a unique people. They were-- it was a poor area. The people-- the Jewish people were working the fields. And he was looking for people that can toil. Actually, when they came, they looked if people had had callouses on their hands. And that's how he picked out a few families in that time, and took them to what at that time was Palestine, already in 1928 and '29.

speaker: interviewee
text: I wanted-- before we go on too far, I wanted to ask you about your bar mitzvah. What was that like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Bar mitzvah. It wasn't a custom in our area. And I wrote about this here. Like, we have a custom to make a big-- a big party-- a big splash of a bar mitzvah. A bar mitzvah-- when I was bar mitzvah, it happened-- you usually call up a boy to the Torah for the first time in his life, when he becomes thirteen years old. And it's done-- it could be done-- there are two days in the week-- on the Monday, and on Thursday-- that there is done reading in the morning from the Torah. Normally, on Sunday and on Tuesday and Wednesday, there is not-- nothing is read in the Torah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it has to be on either a Monday or a Thursday. The parents-- the parents-- the father brought in a bottle of whiskey, and some kichel. Do you know what kichel are? You know, I just saw in today's paper, when I looked this morning, that Leah [? Roston-- ?] the famous Yiddish writer that lived in Philadelphia-- that he just passed away. He is 88 years of age. He writes how the kichel was upstaged. That for hundreds of years, Jews celebrated thier bar mitzvahs and other occasions with a kichel. This was a cookie-- cookies and a-- and a-- and a bottle of schnapps. And it's-- and it's customary that the father makes a [YIDDISH] which says thank God that my son reached an age that I'm not the responsible for his sins anymore. And that's the whole-- that was the whole bar mitzvah. Now I see a lot of people-- when we first came here, and he saw what was going on making bar mitzvahs-- one of my cousins that lives in New York, and-- and-- and he wasn't barely making a living. And he had to borrow money to make a bar mitzvah. I thought it was out of place. And it's--

speaker: interviewee
text: Did your mother come? And your s-- just your father.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, just the father. I was called up in the morning. I was called up to the Torah. And that was it. That was the--

speaker: interviewee
text: What was the religious life in the home like?

speaker: interviewee
text: What do you mean by religious life?

speaker: interviewee
text: Did you celebrate all of the holidays?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, of course. The holidays-- especially, especially the holiday Pesach. Started celebrating-- the preparation was more than-- more than a month. You know, Purim is a month before Pesach. And that's exactly when they-- they-- they-- they used to start cleaning out. The [NON-ENGLISH], the houses-- it-- it-- this was preparing-- now-- now, you go into the store and you buy Manischewitz matzohs for Pesach. Now, this-- this wasn't-- this-- people would-- would get together and-- and bake their own. And this was a very jolly for Rachel. Usually the youth-- young people would help in the baking and the kneading, and it was very strictly supervised.

speaker: interviewee
text: Where would they bake the matzoh?

speaker: interviewee
text: They would-- in the villages, every house had its own oven. There was nobody-- nobody-- very few people bought-- bought-- go went to the store to buy baked bread. They baked their own. They kneaded their own-- their own dough, and they made-- and of course, for Passover, they would prepare the ovens. Everything had to be according to the strictest-- strictest religious-- but it was a joyful thing. And it lasted. It lasted-- it started up between Purim and Pesach. Oh, it lasted a month.

speaker: interviewee
text: Were coming to the end of this tape, now. And is there anything you want to say at the end? Do you want to finish any thought before we get to the next tape?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, let me think. Let me think. I think we'd better finish this tape, and let me think.
